coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and optical properties of luminescent carbon dots from Haloxylon seeds
coredata.dc:creator: Alassafi, Jamaan E.
Al-Hadeethi, Yas
Salah Aida, Mohammed
Fayez Al-Shehri, Samar
coredata.dc:description: In this work, local Haloxylon seeds were utilized as a carbon source to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) using the Pyrolysis method. The morphology, structure, and optical properties of CDs were extensively analyzed. The obtained CDs exhibit a spherical shape with an average diameter of 7.7 nm. They are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen, which formed various hydrophilic functional groups on the surface resulting in enhancement of their water solubility. The CDs emit a bright blue fluorescence under excitation wavelength at 320 nm, with an emission peak at 425 nm and a quantum yield of 1.6 %. The emission characteristics of the CDs is a result of the radiative recombination of the carbon-core state and surface defects, which is dependent on the excitation. This research offers an efficient and environmentally-friendly method for synthesizing luminescent CDs from renewable sources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Haloxylon seeds
Blue emission
Pyrolysis method
Green sources

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The process of synthesizing CDs using Haloxylon seeds. (a-c) Heated dried seed powder at 200 °C for 3 h, resulting in black powder that dissolved in high purity water. (d-e) Filtered and centrifuged the solution to remove unreacted particles. (f) The freeze-dried powder dissolved in distilled water and emitted blue light under 365 nm UV light.

(a) TEM image of CDs (b) High resolution TEM demonstrating dark spot cluster without indicating any sign of graphitic structure as it shown in inset images at the top and bottom. (c) Histogram shows a size distribution of CDs with average size of 7.7 nm. (d) XRD pattern shows a broad diffraction peak at 16° (2θ angle) corresponding to a highly amorphous structure.

(a) FT-IR spectra of the CDs shows their surface is rich with oxygen-related function groups. (b) The XPS survey shows they are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen. (c) High-Resolution XPS of C 1 s, (d) High-Resolution XPS of O 1 s.

(a) Excitation-emission color map of CDs. (b) optical absorption curve. (c) deconvoluted of PL spectra of CDs at excitation wavelengths of 320 nm. (d) Photoluminescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths that shows a excitation-dependent emission property.

(a) a schematic representation of the proposed structure of CDs made of amorphous core structure encapsulated inside a rich shell layer of oxygen-related functional groups. (b) A proposed PL mechanism of excitation-dependent emission.
The parameters used for the determination of the quantum yield of CDs in high purity water under ambient conditions at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and optical properties of luminescent carbon dots from Haloxylon seeds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Sciences, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah 21589, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Sciences, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah 21589, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, local Haloxylon seeds were utilized as a carbon source to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) using the Pyrolysis method. The morphology, structure, and optical properties of CDs were extensively analyzed. The obtained CDs exhibit a spherical shape with an average diameter of 7.7 nm. They are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen, which formed various hydrophilic functional groups on the surface resulting in enhancement of their water solubility. The CDs emit a bright blue fluorescence under excitation wavelength at 320 nm, with an emission peak at 425 nm and a quantum yield of 1.6 %. The emission characteristics of the CDs is a result of the radiative recombination of the carbon-core state and surface defects, which is dependent on the excitation. This research offers an efficient and environmentally-friendly method for synthesizing luminescent CDs from renewable sources.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has revolutionized materials science, leading to the development of innovative materials with unique properties and promising applications. Among these, Carbon Dots (CDs) are a novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials that have captured global attention due to their small sizes, tunable optical properties, and remarkable structure features [1,2]. Their emission can be tuned to emit light across the visible to near-infrared spectra by manipulating their sizes, shapes, and chemical compositions making them valuable for applications in imaging, sensing and so on [2–7].

CDs are small spherical nanoparticles (<10 nm) made of carbon atoms with different functional groups on their surfaces [8]. They are formed by a core–shell structure. Based on their core structure and precursor, they can be classified into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs). GQDs and CQDs made of a crystalline core structure while CNDs lack this property. Notably, despite the contrasting structural properties exhibited by CQDs and CNDs, both are made from organic molecules, while GQDs can be made from graphite [9]. The core structure of CDs is often enveloped with oxygen and nitrogen functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, which contribute to their solubility and versatility [10–12].

Recently, there has been a significant focus on synthesizing CDs from sustainable and eco-friendly sources such as plants and their derivatives [5,13–15]. Various bottom-up synthesis methods have been employed to fabricate CDs from fruits, leaves, and seeds including hydrothermal, microwave- plasma-enhanced decomposition, ultrasonic, solvothermal and even at ambient temperature [5,13-18]. For example, CDs extracted from garlic prepared by hydrothermal technique used for biological imaging [1,13] and fenugreek seeds by a microwave plasma-enhanced decomposition (MPED) for florescent lysozyme protein crystals [19] and so on [8,9–12]. Although these synthesis methods are simple and rapid, they required complicated experimental setups and high energy consumption for the carbonization processes.

In this study, we explored a green, environmentally friendly, water-soluble and emissive CDs from Haloxylon seeds as a sole carbon precursor by simple and one-step pyrolysis method. The structural, chemical composition and optical properties of CDs were investigated in detail. The prepared CDs displayed amorphous core structure and emitted a dark-blue light that depends on excitation wavelength. This method offers a straightforward, rapid, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly approach to convert green sources into value-added product of emissive CDs.
The local Haloxylon seeds were collected from Al-Qassim desert in Saudi Arabia and used without any modification or purification. High purity water was used for the experiment.

Haloxylon seed is a rich source of carbohydrates, and it is locally used in traditional medicine for the treatment of diabetes. To date and to the best of our knowledge, Haloxylon seed has never been used as a source of CDs.

Here, CDs were fabricated in one simple step by pyrolysis method at 473.15 Kelvin (200 0C) for 3 h as demonstrated in Fig. 1 . First, 3 g of dried seeds of Haloxylon were rinsed with tap water and distilled water to remove dust and dirt before being placed in an oven and heated at a fixed temperature of 200 °C for three hours. After cooling, the collected black powder was dissolved in 100 ml of high-purity water in a glass beaker and stirred for 15 min. The resulting yellowish solution was then filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the large unreacted particles. The filtered solution was then subjected to vacuum freeze-drying procedure to obtain the CDs powder. After that, the resulting powder was dissolved in high-purity water and exhibited a blue emission at 425 nm. Finally, the CDs solution was transferred to a glass vial and stored in the dark at room temperature for further characterization.

A transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to analyze the morphology and size distribution of CDs. The procedure consists simply of adding a drop casting of CDs on a 200-mesh thin carbon-coated copper grid and left to dry at ambient temperature before being analyzed with the TEM model Jem-2100f (Jeol, Tokyo, Japan) operated at 200 kV.

The crystallinity structure was studied by using X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) on an Ultima-IV system with Cu X-ray tube (
λ
= 0.1541 nm) that operates at a tube potential of 40 kV and tube current of 40 mA. The scanning speed was set at 4° min−1, covering an angle range of 10° to 80° (with 0.02° interval).

The surface chemical composition of CDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), model K-Alpha Plus (Thermo, USA) and the data obtained were fitted by OriginPro software using the Gaussian function and Shirley background subtraction. A further examination of the surface composition was carried out with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) using a Nicolet iS FTIR spectrometer (Thermo, USA).

The photoluminescence emissions of CDs were examined with a fluorescence spectrophotometer F-7000 (Hitachi, Japan). Absorption spectra were recorded with a UV–visible spectroscopy model UV-3600 (Shimadzu, Japan). All measurements of absorbance and fluorescence spectra were conducted using a quartz cuvette with a pathlength of 10 mm at ambient temperature.

A single point of relative method was used to calculate the quantum yield of the CDs [20]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 N H2SO4 was used as a reference standard because it has similar absorption and emission wavelengths. All measured solutions were prepared with concentrations lower than 0.1 to minimize reabsorption. Then, photoluminescence spectra were measured and the intensities were integrated as shown in Table 1 . The QY of the CDs dissolved in high purity water and excited at 320 nm was calculated using following equation (1):
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
×
G S
G R
×
A S
A R
×
η S
2
η R
2
where Φ, G, A and η are quantum yield, integrated fluorescence intensity, absorbance and refractive index of the solvent (1.33 for H2SO4 and 1.33 for water), respectively. The subscript R refers to the reference standard with known quantum yield and the terms with the subscript S indicate the synthesized CDs.

The morphology, size distribution and structure of CDs were studied by the high-resolution TEM and X-ray diffraction (XRD) as shown in Fig. 2 . Unlike crystalline CDs, amorphous carbon dots do not possess long-range order and have atoms arranged randomly and non-repetitively. This lack of crystallinity is evident in experimental techniques such as HR-TEM, which displays a dark spot nanocluster without clear lattice spacing. This can be further confirmed by the XRD technique where the X-ray waves scattering off the irregular and non-repeating carbon structures within the dots resulting in a broad and featureless halo pattern without sharp peaks in region of d-spacing less than 0.32 nm of graphite structure [21–23].

Fig. 2b displays a HR-TEM image that exhibits carbon nanoparticles of spherical shapes with an average size of 7.7 nm as demonstrated in Fig. 2c. The core structure of these CDs lacks a clear lattice structure, indicating an amorphous structure. This is demonstrated by the upper inset image, which is a high-resolution image showing dark spots of CDs without clear lattice fringes. The corresponding Fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern in the lower inset confirms this, as it exhibits a clear circular shape without clear diffraction fringes [22,24].

The XRD technique was utilized to confirm the core structure of CDs. It effectively distinguishes between core and surface signals, revealing only core structure information. The XRD patterns in Fig. 2c displays a broad diffraction peak at 16°, corresponding to an interlayer spacing distance of 0.55 nm, indicating a highly amorphous structure [22,25–27]. This complements HR-TEM data and confirms the core of CDs are disordered and structurally amorphous.
Chemical compositions and functional groups of the as prepared CDs were studied by Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopies and X ray photoelectron (XPS).

The FT-IR spectra of carbon dots containing oxygen typically reveals distinctive absorption bands corresponding to various functional groups present on the surface of the carbon dots. The oxygen-containing functional groups play a crucial role in determining the chemical and surface properties of the CD.

The FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 3 a clearly reveals the presence of different oxygen-related functional groups at the surface of CDs. An absorption peak centered at 3305 cm−1 was identified as an O–H bond, which enhanced their water solubility [28]. Similarly, stretching peaks at 1613 cm−1 caused by C = O stretching vibration of the carboxylic group and peak at 1414 cm−1 associated with C = C stretching vibration which indicates the formation and stabilization of carbon core structure [16,29]. In addition, peaks centered at 1094 cm -1 could be assigned to C-O and C - OH groups, respectively. The sharp peak at 650 cm−1 is probably assignable to aromatic C–H out of plane bending vibration of benzene nuclei in the benzene ring [29]. Overall, the FT-IR revealed the formation of carbonized core structure that embedded inside highly polar oxygen related function groups.

The XPS spectrum shows that the surface layer of the CDs is mainly composed of carbon (62.5 %) and oxygen (37.5 %) as shown in Fig. 3b. No other elements were detected, indicating the high purity of the carbon source used. The C 1 s peak can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.5 eV (33.1 %) corresponding to the sp2 graphite structure C = C/C–C, while the dominant peak at 286.5 eV (41.5 %) assigned to the sp3 of C-O and another small peak at 288.3 eV (25.4 %) that could be assigned to C = O [25,30,31]. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s can be deconvoluted into three peaks. The main peak at 533 eV (53.2 %) followed by smaller peak at 532.5 eV (34.6 %) and a minor peak at 531.5 eV (12.2 %), correspond to C-O, C = O and O-ads, respectively [32,33].
The PL excitation-emission map (Fig. 4 a), the absorption curve (Fig. 4b) and emission spectrum (Fig. 4c) are all performed under normal conditions and at room temperature.
The PL excitation-emission map of CDs shows a dark-blue emission at 425 nm accompanied by red-shifting the emission upon changing the excitation wavelengths toward higher wavelengths.

UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy reveals the presence of two absorption peaks within the UV range. A minor peak at 230 nm that could be attributed to the π π* transition of the carbon core (C = C) and a dominant peak at 295 nm that can be ascribed to the n-π* transition of the surface containing oxygen related functional groups, mainly (C-O) [10,34–36]. Besides these two bands, a long tail extending into the visible range of the spectrum can be ascribed to lower energy surface centers [36,37]. The inset photo shows CDs emitting blue light when exposed to UV light at 320 nm. The unexposed part remains transparent, indicating the formation of CDs.The inset photograph depicts a blue emission when excited at wavelength of 320 nm, while the unirradiated part displays a transparent solution, confirming the presence of CDs.

The photoluminescence spectrum of CDs Fig. 4c exhibits asymmetric emission peaks at 425 nm, with a broad bandwidth of FWHM at 106 nm and low quantum yields of 1.6 % when excited at a wavelength of 320 nm. The wide FWHM and low quantum yields observed are consistent with previous studies on CDs derived from natural sources and our work is no different [30,38,39]. Deconvolution of the PL spectrum shows two active emissive centers: a small peak at 398 nm (3.1 eV) and a larger peak at 442 nm (2.8 eV). The 44 nm (0.3 eV) difference in energy level between these peaks represents the difference in energy levels of the emissive centers. This also suggested that the overall emission of the CDs is mainly originated from multi-type electronic transitions.

The origin of photoluminescence in CDs is still uncertain, and there is no universal mechanism [36,40–43]. The most accepted explanations involve emission features that are either independent or dependent on excitation [44,45]. The independent emission can be explained by a single electronic transition related to the presence of fluorophores and fully passivated ultra-small CDs [46–49]. On the other hand, excitation-dependent emission can be explained by quantum confinement, heteroatom electronegativity, and surface trapping [44,44]. In this case, the emission peaks shift towards longer wavelengths with a gradual decrease in intensity as the excitation wavelength increases.

Fig. 4d shows the PL spectrum of CDs that exhibited an excitation-dependent emission characteristic. It shows asymmetric emission peaks ranging from 340 to 600 nm, with the emission red-shifting to lower energy as excitation wavelengths changed from 240 to 400 nm. The emission intensities first increased with increasing excitation wavelengths reaching their maximum at excitation wavelength of 320 nm and then gradually decreased toward longer wavelengths.

In fact, the existence of oxygen-related functional groups on the surface layer and core structure of CDs have the ability to introduce their own discrete energy levels within the main energy gap, resulting in additional recombination pathways besides band-to-band recombination [10,35,36].

Combining all these characterization data, the proposed structural model and PL mechanism are presented in Fig. 5 . Therefore, CDs consist of amorphous core encapsulated inside a shell layer that is rich in oxygen-related functional groups. When electrons are excited at wavelengths between 240 and 320 nm, they can be deactivated by vibrational relaxation and finally radiative recombination [10]. In contrast, the energy required for π-π* transition cannot be accomplished with an excitation wavelength longer than 320 nm, and instead electrons are only excited to defect states within the bandgap and result in low-energy emission from the defect states.

In summary, we have demonstrated a simple, eco-friendly and cost-effective method of fabricating CDs from local Haloxylon seeds using Pyrolysis. The obtained CDs have a spherical shape with an average diameter of 7.7 nm and are primarily composed of carbon and oxygen. This composition leads to the formation of various hydrophilic functional groups on the surface, thereby increasing their water solubility. Their blue emission is a result of the radiative recombination of the carbon-core state and surface defects, which is dependent on the excitation. Future research should focus on improving the quantum yield and understanding the effect of reaction conditions on the structure and PL properties of the CDs.

Jamaan E. Alassafi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yas Al-Hadeethi: Writing – original draft, Validation. Mohammed Salah Aida: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization. Samar Fayez Al-Shehri: Writing – original draft, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
